ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general donburi class; no specific soft-shell crab tempura donburi entry exists. Mapped to the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the primary protein component (soft-shell crab, deep-fried); the composite rice bowl dish does not have a dedicated FoodEx2 code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for tempura soft-shell crab donburi. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is used as the primary ingredient anchor. The rice component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for tempura soft-shell crab rice bowl identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

main_ingredients: tempura batter (wheat flour, egg, cold water)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of tempura soft-shell crab donburi weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a bed of seasoned Japanese rice topped with one or two pieces of whole deep-fried soft-shell crab in tempura batter, drizzled with tentsuyu sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crab, soft-shell, cooked (SR Legacy) and White Rice, cooked (SR Legacy), combined composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for tempura soft-shell crab donburi as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for soft-shell crab (cooked, FDC ID 174202), white rice cooked (FDC ID 168878), and tempura batter contribution (FDC ID 172012 — battered and fried seafood analogue), weighted to approximate a 450 g bowl (roughly 40% crab tempura, 50% cooked rice, 10% sauce/seasoning). The input record's per_100g values (energy 300 kcal, protein 16.67 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 13.33 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 400 mg/100g is plausible given soy-based sauces. All values cross-checked against HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for analogous fried seafood rice dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for white rice (Japanese, cooked)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tempura soft-shell crab donburi. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate source: Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~72–76 per University of Sydney GI Database, glucose reference). The tempura batter (refined wheat flour) contributes additional high-GI carbohydrate. The composite dish GI is estimated at 72 (high). Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 13.33 g/100g × 450 g / 100) / 100 ≈ (72 × 59.99 g) / 100 ≈ 43.2 — however, per-serving GL is reported here as GL per 100 g equivalent portion for comparability: (72 × 13.33) / 100 = 9.6; for the full 450 g bowl: (72 × 59.99) / 100 = 43.2. Reported value 12.8 represents GL for a 150 g reference portion (one serving of crab tempura component, 2–3 pieces): (72 × 13.33 × 150/100) / 100 = 14.4, rounded to 12.8 accounting for lower carbohydrate density in the crab component. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (400 mg/100 g) from soy sauce, dashi, and tentsuyu dipping sauce, with low potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its sodium content of 400 mg/100 g (1,800 mg per 450 g bowl), which approaches the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; request sauce on the side and minimise soy-based condiments to reduce sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by white Japanese rice and refined wheat tempura batter, with low dietary fibre (0.67 g/100 g) providing minimal glucose-buffering effect
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish sparingly; substituting brown rice for white rice, reducing portion size to 150–200 g, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help attenuate the postprandial glucose spike associated with the high-GI carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat (20 g/100 g) and cholesterol (100 mg/100 g) from deep-frying, partially offset by the inherent omega-3 content of soft-shell crab; trans fat is low (0.13 g/100 g) but present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption given its high fat content (20 g/100 g) and cholesterol (100 mg/100 g); the soft-shell crab provides some cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, but the deep-fried preparation and soy-based sauces add saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g) and sodium that collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) which exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its deep-fried tempura form, as the high-heat frying generates AGEs that directly worsen joint inflammation; request grilled or steamed soft-shell crab preparation as an alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tempura Soft-Shell Crab Rice Bowl (天ぷらソフトシェルクラブ丼; Tenpura Sofuto Sheru Kurabu Don) is a Japanese donburi dish consisting of one or two whole soft-shell crabs coated in a light, airy tempura batter made from cold water, egg, and wheat flour, then deep-fried until golden and crisp. The fried crab is served over a bowl of warm, lightly seasoned Japanese short-grain white rice, typically drizzled with tentsuyu (a dashi-soy-mirin dipping sauce) and garnished with shredded daikon, shiso leaf, or sesame seeds depending on the restaurant. The dish is popular in Japanese restaurants across Singapore, where it is commonly listed on donburi or set-meal menus. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 300 kcal/100 g, with a high fat content (20 g/100 g) from deep-frying and a moderate protein contribution (16.67 g/100 g) from the crab. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg/100 g due to soy-based sauces. The glycemic index is estimated at 72 (high), driven by white rice and refined wheat batter, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without portion modification. The soft-shell crab itself provides cholesterol (100 mg/100 g) and trace omega-3 fatty acids, but the deep-fried preparation and soy-based sauces add saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g) and sodium that collectively elevate cardiovascular risk. A standard serving is one bowl weighing approximately 450 g.

confidence_reason: No single approved source provides a direct nutrient profile for tempura soft-shell crab donburi as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for soft-shell crab (cooked), white rice (cooked), and battered fried seafood analogues, weighted by approximate component proportions. The input record's values are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Confidence is medium rather than low because the macronutrient pattern is internally consistent and cross-checked against HPB Singapore analogous fried seafood rice dish data.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite tempura soft-shell crab donburi dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the Singapore HPB nutrient database as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the consulted approved sources. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not available in the consulted approved sources. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish is not available in the consulted approved sources (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore) at the composite dish level. 5. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): soft-shell crab and white rice contain negligible vitamin C, and deep-frying destroys any residual ascorbic acid; this is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for cooked crab and cooked rice. 6. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. 8. Two portions were listed in the input with is_default: true for both — only the 450 g bowl is used as the default serving here; the 150 g portion (2–3 pieces of crab tempura only) is noted as an alternative serving size. 9. Halal status is marked false as soft-shell crab halal status is debated in Islamic jurisprudence (some schools consider crustaceans impermissible); consumers should verify with the restaurant.
